summary: Life in Burma was very difficult for me and my family. Our house was burned and our farm destroyed by Burmese soldiers during the battles between the Karen and Burmese forces. We were forced to live in temporary villages and were not allowed to own any property. Rice, our main food, became unaffordable as fewer people worked the farms due to ongoing conflicts. These hardships eventually led my parents to escape Burma in 1997.

After fleeing Burma, my family lived in a refugee camp in Thailand starting in 1997. We stayed there for over twelve years, receiving support from the United Nations such as food, shelter, and clothing. Life in the Thai refugee camp was challenging, but it was a safe place compared to the battles we left behind. It was in Thailand that we awaited the opportunity to resettle in the United States.

The United Nations played a crucial role in my family's journey. While we were in the Thai refugee camp, they provided us with essential support like food, shelter, and clothing. In 2000, the United Nations allowed Karen refugees to resettle in the United States, which gave my family hope for a better future. They also conducted background checks and medical exams before our resettlement interview in 2007, helping us transition safely to a new life.

The International Organization for Migration was important to my family just before we left for the United States. The day before our departure, they gave us a special bag that helped us carry our belongings and symbolized our new beginning. This organization helped facilitate our move from the Thai refugee camp to the United States, making the transition smoother for us.

The United States gave my family a chance to start a new and better life after years of hardship. We arrived after passing interviews and medical checks arranged by the United Nations and other organizations. Life here is wonderful and much easier than in Burma; we enjoy freedom, education, and equal rights. I am grateful for the opportunity to live safely and build a future in this country.

I now live in Minnesota with my family, and I feel safe and at home here. Minnesota has given us the freedom and opportunities we never had in Burma or the refugee camp. Being in Minnesota means I can be with my family and pursue education and a better life. I always remember the struggles we endured to get here and am thankful for this new chapter.
